description: The presented image is irrelevant to the Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel research described in the supporting paper metadata. It displays a software interface button with the text "Check for updates" and a graphic of a red bookmark inside a partial yellow-blue circular ring. No material science related content including microstructure characterization images, mechanical property curves, phase identification results, or processing schematics associated with the target steel study can be identified from this image, so no valid scientific information to support the paper's conclusions on Cr addition effects on microstructure and mechanical properties of the low-density steel can be extracted from the current visual content.

This figure displays X-ray diffraction patterns of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steel strips, with x values of 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.% corresponding to samples labeled 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr respectively. The x-axis represents 2θ ranging from 30° to 100°, while the y-axis denotes diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). All observed peaks are indexed to two phases: face-centered cubic austenite (γ) with corresponding crystallographic planes (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), and body-centered cubic δ-ferrite with planes (110), (200), (211). As Cr content increases, the intensity of the δ(110) peak at 2θ = 44.1° gradually rises, proving that Cr addition promotes δ-ferrite formation. Calculated austenite lattice constants decrease from 3.70 Å for 0Cr to 3.55 Å for 9Cr. This figure confirms that austenite is the major matrix phase for all as-cast samples, and Cr addition effectively increases ferrite fraction in the alloy.

This group of optical micrographs shows the microstructure of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr austenitic low-density steel strips, with subfigures (a), (b), (c), (d) corresponding to x=0, 3, 6, 9 wt.% Cr respectively, all with a scale bar of 20 μm. The microstructure consists of austenite as the matrix phase and δ-ferrite uniformly distributed inside austenite grains. With increasing Cr content, the volume fraction of ferrite rises gradually from 10.2 vol% (0Cr sample) to 19.6 vol% (9Cr sample), and the ferrite morphology evolves from rod-like to block-like. The observation that ferrite locates inside austenite grains confirms that δ-ferrite nucleates and grows from the melt first during near-rapid solidification, which matches the FA (ferrite-to-austenite) solidification mode of the alloy system, supporting the conclusion that Cr addition promotes ferrite formation in this low-density steel.

This figure presents the tensile properties of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr austenitic low-density steels, where x equals 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.% Cr (denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr respectively, with 4 curves corresponding to the four compositions in both subplots). Subplot (a) plots engineering stress (in MPa, 0-1000 range) against engineering strain (in %, 0-50 range). Subplot (b) presents true stress (in MPa, 0-1500 range, left y-axis) and strain hardening rate (in MPa, 1000-4000 range, right y-axis) as functions of true strain (0-0.4 range). Key trends observed: as Cr content increases, ultimate tensile strength increases (from ~900 MPa for 0Cr to ~1000 MPa for 9Cr) while elongation to fracture decreases (from ~49% for 0Cr to ~25% for 9Cr); the strain hardening rate at equivalent true strain rises significantly with higher Cr addition, and the 0Cr alloy shows the lowest and fastest-declining strain hardening rate during deformation. The figure supports that Cr addition improves the strength and strain hardening ability of the tested low-density steels but reduces their tensile ductility.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterizations of austenite in Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6, 9 wt.%) near-rapid solidified strips. Subfigures (a)-(d) are bright-field (BF) TEM images with corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns (SADPs) for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips respectively: the 0Cr strip shows low dislocation density and clear κ-carbide superlattice spots in its SADP; dislocation density increases with Cr addition, reaching the highest level in the 6Cr strip, while κ-carbide superlattice spots turn weak in 3Cr/6Cr strips and disappear in the 9Cr strip. Subfigure (e) is a dark-field (DF) TEM image of the 0Cr strip, revealing κ-carbides at the austenite-ferrite phase boundary. Subfigure (f) is a BF TEM image of the 9Cr strip with corresponding SADP, showing granular/banded Cr₂₃C₆ precipitates (~100 nm width) at austenite grain boundaries. These results confirm that Cr addition increases austenite dislocation density, suppresses κ-carbide precipitation, and induces Cr₂₃C₆ formation at high Cr content, which accounts for the observed mechanical property changes of the alloys.

This set of figures presents dark-field (DF) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns (SADPs) of δ-ferrite in Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.%) austenitic low-density steel strips, with a uniform scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (a) shows the DF image of 0Cr steel captured using (110)δ reflection, whose SADP corresponds to [111]δ zone axis, with no superlattice spots and clean ferrite without evident secondary phases. Subfigure (b) is the DF image of 3Cr steel using $(1\overline{1}0)_\delta$ reflection, also with [111]δ zone axis SADP and no observable secondary phases in ferrite. Subfigures (c) (6Cr steel, using $(200)_{DO3}$ reflection) and (d) (9Cr steel, using $(200)_{DO3}$ reflection) show SADPs with superlattice spots belonging to DO3 phase, corresponding to [001]δ+[001]DO3 and [011]δ+[011]DO3 zone axes respectively, and abundant nano-sized DO3 precipitates distributed in ferrite. The figure confirms that Cr addition ≥6 wt.% promotes DO3 ordered phase precipitation in δ-ferrite of the investigated steel, while no such ordered phase forms in 0Cr and 3Cr steels.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) austenitic low-density steel strips after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h. The x-axis represents 2θ ranging from 30° to 100°, and the y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units. Four stacked curves correspond to 0Cr (black), 3Cr (blue), 6Cr (red), and 9Cr (green) samples respectively. Identified phases include face-centered cubic austenite (γ, indexed to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222) peaks), body-centered cubic δ-ferrite (indexed to (110), (200), (211) peaks), and κ-carbide (κ(111) peak at ~41.4°). The κ-carbide peak is only observed in the 0Cr sample, while it is completely absent in all Cr-containing samples. All samples retain austenite and δ-ferrite as primary constituent phases after annealing. The results demonstrate that Cr addition effectively suppresses κ-carbide precipitation in this steel system even after the 550 °C 5 h annealing treatment.

This set of SEM micrographs shows the microstructure of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steel strips after annealing at 550 ℃ for 5 h, where subplots (a), (b), (c), (d) correspond to Cr content x=0, 3, 6, 9 wt.% respectively, all with a 20 μm scale bar. Clear morphology differences are observed: the 0Cr sample (a) exhibits distinct grain boundaries and dendritic structures, while grain boundaries are unobservable in all Cr-containing samples (b, c, d) under the same observation condition, indicating Cr addition improves the corrosion resistance of the steel strips. Combined with supporting XRD results, this figure confirms that Cr addition suppresses the precipitation of κ-carbides in the alloy even after 550 ℃ annealing, supporting the core conclusion that Cr regulates the phase precipitation and corrosion performance of this low-density steel system.

This figure presents tensile properties of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) austenitic low-density steel strips annealed at 550 °C for 5 h. Subplot (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves, with x-axis as engineering strain (%) and y-axis as engineering stress (MPa). Subplot (b) shows true stress-strain curves (left y-axis: true stress, MPa; x-axis: true strain) and corresponding strain hardening rate curves (right y-axis: strain hardening rate, MPa). Four samples are tested, denoted as 0Cr (black solid line), 3Cr (red dashed line), 6Cr (blue dotted line), and 9Cr (cyan dash-dot line). Key trends include: both engineering and true tensile strength increase with rising Cr content; strain hardening rate of annealed strips is positively correlated with Cr content. Except for 9Cr, annealed samples show reduced strain hardening rate compared to as-cast counterparts, with 3Cr exhibiting the largest reduction of ~500 MPa. This figure validates that Cr addition effectively modulates the tensile mechanical performance of the studied low-density steels.

This group of TEM images characterizes the austenite microstructure of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.%) austenitic low-density steel after annealing at 550 ℃ for 5 h. Subfigures (a)-(d) are bright-field (BF) TEM images of 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr strips respectively, each paired with corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns (SADP), where dislocations in austenite matrix are marked. SADPs confirm orientation relationships: [001]γ+[001]κ for 0Cr, [011]γ for 3Cr, [112]γ+[112]κ for 6Cr, [001]γ for 9Cr. Subfigure (e) is dark-field (DF) image of 0Cr strip along (010)κ, showing nano-sized κ-carbides distributed in matrix. Subfigure (f) is DF image of 9Cr strip paired with SADP of [114]Cr₂₃C₆, showing ~200 nm Cr₂₃C₆ precipitates. The results demonstrate that increasing Cr content suppresses κ-carbide precipitation: obvious κ-carbide superlattices exist in 0Cr, weak in 3Cr/6Cr, and absent in 9Cr where Cr₂₃C₆ forms instead; dislocation density of annealed Cr-containing steels is lower than as-cast counterparts but higher than annealed 0Cr steel, supporting the correlation between Cr addition, microstructure evolution and mechanical performance.

This group of figures presents dark-field (DF) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns (SADP) of ferrite in Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.%) austenitic low-density steel annealed at 550 °C for 5 h. Subfigure (a) shows the DF image of 0Cr strip collected along $(1\overline{1}0)_\delta$ zone axis, (b) is the DF image of 3Cr strip along $(1\overline{1}0)_\delta$ zone axis, (c) is the DF image of 6Cr strip along $(200)_{\mathrm{DO3}}$ zone axis, (d) is the DF image of 9Cr strip along $(200)_{\mathrm{DO3}}$ zone axis, with a uniform scale bar of 500 nm for all TEM images. SADP results confirm the coexistence of δ ferrite and DO3 ordered phase in ferrite regions of all annealed strips, with orientation relationships of $[001]_\delta//[001]_{\mathrm{DO3}}$ or $[011]_\delta//[011]_{\mathrm{DO3}}$. As Cr content increases from 0 to 9 wt.%, the ferrite morphology evolves from irregular large blocks to finer equiaxed/polyhedral grains. This figure verifies that DO3 ordered phase precipitates in ferrite of all Cr-containing and Cr-free annealed strips, supporting the conclusion that annealing treatment and Cr addition regulate the phase structure and morphology of ferrite in the tested low-density steel.

This figure presents mechanical properties and corresponding microstructure of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C–3Cr austenitic low-density steel annealed at 550 °C for different durations. Subfigure (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for as-cast sample, and samples annealed at 550 °C for 1 h, 5 h, 9 h: plasticity (elongation) increases gradually with longer annealing time, while yield strength remains nearly unchanged after 1 h annealing, and ultimate tensile strength reaches above 900 MPa for all annealed samples. Subfigures (b), (c), (d) are bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of samples annealed for 1 h, 5 h, 9 h respectively, with scale bars of 500 nm, 500 nm, 100 nm. These TEM images show that dislocation density decreases progressively with increasing annealing time, as marked by yellow arrows. The figure supports the conclusion that nano-scale κ-carbides precipitate mainly within the first 1 h of annealing to improve yield strength, while longer annealing enables dislocation recovery to enhance plasticity, so annealing time should be optimized to balance κ-carbide coarsening and dislocation density reduction for optimal mechanical performance.

This set of TEM images display slip band morphologies of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C austenitic low-density steel samples after tensile fracture, covering four sample conditions: (a) as-cast 0Cr steel strip with an average slip band spacing of ~90 nm, (b) annealed 0Cr steel strip with an average slip band spacing of ~190 nm, (c) as-cast 6Cr steel strip with an average slip band spacing of ~80 nm, (d) annealed 6Cr steel strip with an average slip band spacing of ~60 nm. Slip bands are marked with yellow arrows in all subgraphs, each with a 500 nm scale bar. Comparison reveals that annealing increases slip band spacing for 0Cr steel but reduces the spacing for 6Cr steel, which matches the change trend of tensile elongation of these samples. Slip band width is measured to be inversely correlated with slip band spacing. The observed slip band features are associated with κ-carbide precipitation state and initial dislocation density of different samples, supporting that Cr addition and annealing regulate deformation behavior and mechanical properties of the low-density steel.

This chart presents a comparison of three post-tensile fracture parameters for three Fe-28Mn-9Al-1.2C austenitic low-density steel samples: as-cast 0Cr, as-cast 6Cr, and annealed 6Cr. Three indicators are plotted: the black square curve for slip band spacing (unit: nm, left y-axis), the magenta triangular curve for slip band width (unit: nm, right magenta y-axis), and the blue hollow circle curve for total elongation (unit: %, right blue y-axis). Slip band spacing decreases sequentially from ~90 nm (as-cast 0Cr) to ~80 nm (as-cast 6Cr) to ~60 nm (annealed 6Cr), and slip band width follows a matching decreasing trend from ~14 nm to ~12 nm to ~8 nm. Total elongation first drops from ~39% (as-cast 0Cr) to ~29% (as-cast 6Cr), then rises to ~43% (annealed 6Cr). The figure validates that while finer, denser slip bands generally correspond to higher ductility via dynamic slip band refinement, the high pre-deformation dislocation density in as-cast 6Cr lowers its elongation, confirming pre-existing dislocations are a critical factor affecting the ductility of this steel system besides slip band formation ability during deformation.
